title: Word Sense Induction with Knowledge Distillation from BERT

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - WORD SENSE INDUCTION WITH KNOWLEDGE DISTIL LATION FROM BERT

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Pre-trained contextual language models are ubiquitously employed for language understanding tasks, but are unsuitable for resource-constrained systems. Noncontextual word embeddings are an efficient alternative in these settings. Such methods typically use one vector to encode multiple different meanings of a word, and incur errors due to polysemy. This paper proposes a two-stage method to distill multiple word senses from a pre-trained language model (BERT) by using attention over the senses of a word in a context and transferring this sense information to fit multi-sense embeddings in a skip-gram-like framework. We demonstrate an effective approach to training the sense disambiguation mechanism in our model with a distribution over word senses extracted from the output layer embeddings of BERT. Experiments on the contextual word similarity and sense induction tasks show that this method is superior to or competitive with state-of-the-art multi-sense embeddings on multiple benchmark data sets, and experiments with an embedding-based topic model (ETM) demonstrates the benefits of using this multi-sense embedding in a downstream application.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

While modern deep contextual word embeddings have dramatically improved the state-of-the-art in natural language understanding (NLU) tasks, shallow noncontextual representation of words are more practical solution in settings constrained by compute power or latency. In single-sense embeddings such as word2vec or GloVe, the different meanings of a word are represented by the same vector, which leads to the meaning conflation problem in the presence of polysemy. Arora et al. (2018) showed that in the presence of polysemy, word embeddings are linear combination of the different sense embeddings. This leads to another limitation in the word embeddings due to the triangle inequality of vectors (Neelakantan et al., 2014). For _apple_ , the distance between the words similar to its two meanings, i.e. _peach_ and _software_ , will be less than the sum of their distances to _apple_ . To account for these and other issues that arise due to polysemy, several multi-sense embeddings model have been proposed to learn multiple vector representations for each word.

There are two major components for a multi-sense embedding model to be used in downstream tasks: (1) _sense selection_ to find the correct sense of a word in context (2) _sense representation_ for the different meanings of a word. Two types of tasks are used to evaluate these two distinct abilities of a word sense embedding model: word sense induction and contextual word similarity respectively. In word sense induction, a model has to select the correct meaning of a word given its context. For contextual word similarity, the model must be able to embed word senses with similar meaning close to each other in the embedding space. Prior works on multi-sense embeddings have not been shown to be effective on these two tasks simultaneously (e.g. AdaGram (Bartunov et al., 2016) works well on word sense induction but not on contextual word similarity). We propose a method to distill knowledge from a contextual embedding model, BERT (Devlin et al., 2018), for learning a word sense model that can perform both tasks effectively. Furthermore, we show the advantage of sense embeddings over word embeddings for training topic models.

Pre-trained language models like BERT, GPT (Radford et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) etc. have been immensely successful in multiple natural language understanding tasks, but the computational requirements and large latencies of these models inhibits their use in low-resource systems. Static word embeddings or sense embeddings have significantly lower computational

requirements and lower latencies compared to these large transformer models. In this work, we use knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015) to transfer the contextual information in BERT for improving the learning of multi-sense word embedding models. Although there are several prior works on distilling knowledge to smaller transformer models, to the best of our knowledge there has been no work on using knowledge distillation to improve the training of word or sense embeddings using pre-trained language models.

The contributions of this paper are:

- (i) A two-stage method for knowledge distillation from BERT to static word sense embeddings
- (ii) A sense embedding model with state-of-the-art simultaneous performance on sense selection and sense induction.
- (iii) A demonstration of the increased efficacy of using these multi-sense embedding in lieu of single sense in embeddings in a downstream task, namely embedded topic modeling (Dieng et al., 2020).

## 2 RELATED WORK

Here we describe a number of previous models trained to represent different meanings of words using the _word2vec_ framework. Prior works on model distillation using BERT are also discussed.

Reisinger & Mooney (2010) was the first to learn vector representations for multiple senses of a word. They extracted feature vectors of a word in different contexts from the context words and clustered them to generate sense clusters for a word. The cluster centroids are then used as sense embeddings or prototypes. Huang et al. (2012) similarly clustered the context representations of a word using spherical k-means, and learned sense embeddings by training a neural language model.

Neelakantan et al. (2014) extended the skip-gram model to learn the cluster centers the sense embeddings simultaneously. At each iteration, the context embedding is assigned to the nearest cluster center and the sense embedding for that cluster is used to predict the context word. A non-parametric method is employed to learn an appropriate number of clusters during training.

Tian et al. (2014) defined the probability of a context word given a center word as a mixture model with word senses representing the mixtures. They developed an EM algorithm for learning the embeddings by extending the skip-gram likelihood function. Li & Jurafsky (2015) used the Chinese Restaurent Process to learn the number of senses while training the sense embeddings. They evaluated their embeddings on a number of downstream tasks but did not find a consistent advantage in using multi-sense embeddings over single-sense embeddings.

Athiwaratkun & Wilson (2017) represented words as multi-modal Gaussian distributions where the different modes correspond to different senses of a word. To denote the similarity between two word distributions, they used the expected likelihood kernel replacing cosine similarity for vectors.

Bartunov et al. (2016) proposed a non-parameteric Bayesian extension to the skip-gram model to learn the number of senses of words automatically. They used the Dirichlet Process for infinite mixture modeling by assuming prior probabilities over the meanings of a word. Due to the intractibility of the likelihood function, they derived a stochastic variational inference algorithm for the model.

Grzegorczyk & Kurdziel (2018) learned sense disambiguation embeddings to model the probability of different word senses in a context. They used the gumbel-softmax method to approximate the categorical distribution of sense probabilities. To learn the number of senses of a word, they added an entropy loss and combined it with a pruning strategy.

Lee & Chen (2017) proposed the first sense embedding model using a reinforcement learning framework. They learned only sense embeddings and an efficient sense selection module for assigning the correct sense to a word in context.

Hinton et al. (2015) demonstrated the effectiveness of employing a large pre-trained teacher network to guide the training of a smaller student network and named this method **model distillation** . Recently, this technique has been used to train many efficient transformer models from BERT. Sun et al. (2019); Sanh et al. (2019); Jiao et al. (2019); Sun et al. (2020) have trained smaller BERT models by distilling knowledge from pre-trained BERT models down to fewer layers. However, there seems to be no prior work on improving word or sense embeddings by using pre-trained language models like BERT.

## 3.2 TRANSFER KNOWLEDGE FROM BERT

We use the contextual embeddings from BERT to improve our sense embedding model in 2 steps. First, we learn the sense embeddings as a linear decomposition of the BERT contextual embedding in a context window. Second, the sense embeddings from the first step are used for knowledge distillation to our sense embedding model.

## 3.2.2 KNOWLEDGE DISTILLATION FROM BERT

Here we combine our multi-sense embedding model and the sense embeddings from BERT to transfer the contextual information. To be precise, we want to improve the sense disambiguation module (3) of the multi-sense embedding model using BERT. The key idea of knowledge distillation is that the output from a complex network contains more fine grained information than a class label. So we want the student network to produce similar probabilities as the teacher network. Hinton et al. (2015) suggested using a “softer" version of the output from the teacher network using a temperature _T_ over the output logits **z** _i_ :

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We conduct both qualitative and quantitative experiments on our trained sense embedding model.

## 4.1 DATASET AND TRANING

We trained our models on the April 2010 snapshot of Wikipedia (Shaoul & Westbury, 2010) for fair comparison with the prior multi-sense embedding models. This corpus has over 2 million documents and about 990 million words. We also used the same vocabulary set as Neelakantan et al. (2014).

We selected the hyperparameters of our models by training them on a small part of the corpus and using the loss metric on a validation set. For training, we set the context window size to ∆= 5. We use 10 negative samples for each positive (word, context) pair. We do not apply subsampling like word2vec since the BERT model was pre-trained on sentences without subsampling. We learn 3 sense embeddings for each word with a dimension of 300 for comparison with prior work. We use the Adam (Kingma & Ba, 2014) optimizer with a learning rate of 0 _._ 001.

The base model of BERT is used as the pre-trained language model. The PyTorch _transformers_ (Wolf et al., 2019) library is used for inference through the BERT-base model. With a batch size of 2048, it takes 10 hours to train the sense embedding model on the wikipedia corpus for one epoch in a Tesla V100 GPU. The sense embedding models with and without knowledge distillation are trained for 2 epochs over the full Wikipedia dataset. The weight for the knowledge distillation loss (Eq. 9) _α_ = 1 is selected from {0.1, 0.5, 1.0}. Temperature parameter _T_ is set to 4 by tuning over the range [2,6].

## 4.2 NEAREST NEIGHBOURS

The nearest neighbours of words in context are shown in Table 1. Given the context words, the context embedding is computed using Eq. 5 and sense probability distribution is computed with Eq. 3. The most probable sense is selected and then the cosine distance is computed with the global embeddings of all words in the vocabulary. Table 1 also shows the values of the sense probability for the word in context. The sense probabilities are close to 1 in most of the cases. This indicates that the model learned a narrow distribution over the senses and it selects a single sense most of the time.

## 4.3 WORD SENSE INDUCTION

This task evaluates the model’s ability to select the appropriate sense given a context. To assign a sense to a word in context, we take the words in its context window and compute the context embedding using Eq. 5. Then the sense probability from Eq. 3 gives the most probable sense for that context.

We follow Bartunov et al. (2016) for the evaluation dataset and metrics. Three WSI datasets are used in our experiments. The SemEval-2007 dataset is from SemEval-2007 Task 2 competition (Agirre & Soroa, 2007). It contains 27232 contexts for 100 words collected from Wall Street Journal (WSJ) corpus. The SemEval-2010 dataset is from the SemEval-2010 Task 14 competition (Manandhar &

For evaluation, we cluster the different contexts of a word according to the model’s predicted sense. To evaluate the clustering of a set of points, we take pairs of points in the dataset and check if they are in the same cluster according to the ground truth. For the SemEval competitions, two traditional metrics were used: F-score and V-measure. But there are intrinsic biases in these methods. F-score will be higher for a cluster with fewer centers while V-measure will be higher for clusters with a large number of centers. So Bartunov et al. (2016) used ARI (Adjusted Rand Index) as the clustering evaluation method since it does not suffer from these problems.

We report the ARI scores for MSSG (Neelakantan et al., 2014), MPSG (Tian et al., 2014) and AdaGram (Bartunov et al., 2016) from Bartunov et al. (2016). Scores for disambiguated skip-gram are collected from Grzegorczyk & Kurdziel (2018). We used the trained embeddings published by MUSE Lee & Chen (2017) with Boltzmann method for this task. Single-sense models like skip-gram cannot be used in this task since the task is to assign different senses to different contexts of a word. Contextual models like BERT give an embedding for a word given its context, but this embedding is not associated with a specific discrete meaning of the word. Therefore BERT models are not suitable for comparison on this task.

MUSE shows strong performance in word similarity tasks (section 4.4) but fails to induce meaningful senses given a context. The Disambiguated Skip-gram model was the best model on SemEval-2007 and SemEval-2010 datasets. Our sense embedding model performs better than MSSG and MPSG models but there is a large gap with the state-of-the-art. We can see a significant improvement in ARI score (1 _._ 25 _x −_ 2 _._ 25 _x_ ) on the 3 datasets with knowledge distillation from BERT.

## 4.4 CONTEXTUAL WORD SIMILARITY

Here, the task is to predict the similarity of the meaning of 2 words given the context they were used. We use the Stanford Contextual Word Similarity (SCWS) dataset (Huang et al., 2012). This dataset

contains 2003 word pairs with their contexts, selected from Wikipedia. Spearman’s rank correlation _ρ_ is used to measure the degree of agreement between the human similarity scores and the model’s similarity scores.

We use the following methods for predicting similarity of two words in context for a sense embedding model following Reisinger & Mooney (2010): AvgSimC and MaxSimC. The AvgSimC measure weighs the cosine similarity of all combinations of sense embeddings of 2 words with the probability of those 2 senses given their respective contexts.
Here, the cosine similarity between sense _i_ word 1 and sense _j_ of word 2 is multiplied by the probability of these 2 senses given their contexts.
The MaxSimC method selects the best sense for each context based on their probabilities and measures their cosine similarity.

where

For comparison with baselines on this task, we use the single-sense skip-gram model (Mikolov et al., 2013) with 300 and 900 dimensions since our word sense model has 3 embeddings per word with 300 dimensions each. We use the contextual models (BERT, DistilBERT) by selecting the token embedding from the output layer for the word in a context window of 10 words to the left and right.

We report the score for the best performing model from each method in Table 3. For MUSE, the best model (Boltzmann method) is used.[1] . Our sense embedding model (SenseEmbed) is competitive in AvgSimC measure whereas the knowledge distillation method significantly improves the MaxSimC score. We can infer from this result that the improvement in sense induction mechanism helped learn better embeddings for each word sense. The best performing multi-sense embedding model’s performance is very close to the 900 dimensional skip-gram embeddings (69.3 vs 68.4). The best performing multi-sense model considering the average over the 2 measures is MUSE with an average of 68.2. But it does not do well on the word sense induction task. Both contextual embedding models (BERT and DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019)) obtained lower scores than our sense embedding model.

Since the BERT sense embeddings are trained using the output embeddings from BERT, we can use them as context embedding for sense induction.

In the knowledge distillation method, we train our sense embedding model to mimick the sense distribution from the BERT Sense model. Since the WSI task evaluates the predicted sense of the model, this is the ideal task to quantify the quality of knowledge transfer from BERT to the sense embedding model. The BERT Sense model is compared with the sense embedding model trained with knowledge distillation in Table 4. As the BERT Sense model is trained with MSE (Mean Squared Error) loss, it’s sense embeddings have the same dimensionality as BERT (768). From Table 4, we see that the 300 dimensional sense embedding model can obtain 75% to 88% ARI scores without using the 12 layer transformer model with 768 dimensional embeddings.

We also compare the BERT sense model with the sense embedding model on the contextual word similarity task in Table 5. The lower correlation score from BERT Sense model indicates that these embeddings are not better at sense representation although they learn to disambiguate senses. The loss function in Eq. 8 helps the BERT Sense model to extract contextual sense information in BERT output embeddings, but it is not optimal for training sense embeddings. The sense embedding model combined with the knowledge gained from the BERT sense model learns to do both i) sense selection and ii) sense representation effectively.

## 4.6 EMBEDDED TOPIC MODEL

As sense embeddings help discern the meaning of a word in context, prior work has shown the benefits of using them to perform topic modeling in the embedding space (Dieng et al., 2020). We explore the further benefits of using embeddings which model the fact that one token can have multiple meanings. Given a word embedding matrix _ρ_ where the column _ρv_ is the embedding of word _v_ , Embedded Topic Model (ETM) follows the generative process:

1. Draw topic proportions _θd ∼LN_ (0 _, I_ )

2. For each word _n_ in the document:

- (a) Draw topic assignment _zdn ∼_ Cat( _θd_ )

- (b) Draw the word _wdn ∼_ softmax( _ρ[T] αzdn_ )

Here, _αk_ is the topic embedding for topic _k_ . These topic embeddings are used to obtain per-topic distribution over the vocabulary of words.

We experimentally compare the performance of ETM models trained[2] using skip-gram word embeddings, our sense embeddings and MSSG (Neelakantan et al., 2014) embeddings. Three senses were trained for each word in both of the multi-sense models. A 15K document training set, 4.9K test set, and 100 document validation set are sampled from the Wikipedia data set and used for the experiments. During training using our sense embeddings and the MSSG multi-sense embeddings, the sentences are represented using a "bag-of-sense" representation where each word is replaced with its most probable sense , selected using a context window of 10 words; with the skip-gram embeddings, bag-of-words representations are used.
For evaluation, we measured the document completion perplexity, topic coherence, and topic diversity for different vocabulary sizes. Document perplexity measures how well the topic model can be used

to predict the second half of a document, given its first half. Topic coherence measures the probability of the top words in each topic to occur in the same documents, while topic diversity is a measure of the uniqueness of the most common words in each topic. Higher topic coherence and topic diversity indicate that a model performs better at extracting distinct topics; the product of the two measures is used as a measure of topic quality:

## topic quality = topic coherence _×_ topic diversity

We follow (Dieng et al., 2020) in reporting the ratio of perplexity and topic quality as a measure of the performance of the topic model: a lower ratio is better.

We set the minimum document frequency to [5, 10, 30, 100] to change the vocabulary size. ETM is initialized with pre-trained sense embeddings and then fine tuned along with the topic embeddings. We followed the hyperparameters reported in Dieng et al. (2020) to train all models. Since we use a bag-of-sense model, ETM provides a probability distribution over the senses. We sum the probability for the different senses of a word to obtain the probability for a word. In Figure 2, we show the ratio of the document completion perplexity (normalized by vocabulary size) and the topic quality.

We see that both MSSG and BERTKDEmbed ETM models obtain lower perplexity-quality ratio for higher vocabulary sizes compared to the single-sense (skip-gram) ETM model. This confirms that multi-sense embedding models produce Embedded Topic Models with higher performance. BERTKDEmbed ETM also outperforms the other multi-sense ETM model.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We present a multi-sense word embedding model for representing the different meanings of a word using knowledge distillation from a pre-trained language model (BERT). Unlike prior work on model distillation from contextual language models, we focus on extracting non-contextual word senses; this is accomplished in an unsupervised manner using an intermediate attention-based sense disambiguation model. We demonstrate start-of-the-art results on three word sense induction data sets using fast and efficient sense embedding models. This is the first multi-sense word embedding model with strong performance on both the word sense induction and contextual word similarity tasks. As a downstream application, we show that the sense embedding model increases the performance of the recent embedding-based topic model (Dieng et al., 2020). In future, we will explore the benefits of using pre-trained multi-sense embeddings as features for other downstream tasks, and tackle the task of tuning the number of word meanings for each individual word.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a timely and relevant approach to distilling word sense information from BERT into static multi-sense embeddings, addressing a real need for efficient, interpretable representations in low-resource settings. The integration of knowledge distillation with a skip-gram-style framework is conceptually sound, and the empirical evaluation covers standard benchmarks in word sense induction and contextual similarity. The downstream application to topic modeling adds practical value.

Overview issues:

- Lack of ablation isolates distillation effect: The paper does not include an ablation study that isolates the contribution of knowledge distillation from other components of the model, such as the attention-based disambiguation mechanism or the iterative context update. Without comparing the full model to a version trained without BERT supervision, it is unclear whether performance gains come from the distillation signal or from architectural choices. This undermines the core claim that BERT's knowledge is effectively transferred. A proper ablation would compare SenseEmbed (no distillation) directly with BERTKDEmbed using identical hyperparameters and training conditions.

- Context leakage in BERT sense modeling: The method for extracting sense distributions from BERT uses the full context window—including the target word’s neighbors—to compute both the BERT contextual embedding $\mathbf{b}_i$ and the context vector $\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}$ in Eq. 7. Because these are derived from the same corpus context used during training, there is a risk of direct information leakage: the model could exploit surface overlap rather than learning generalizable sense disambiguation. This is especially problematic given that the SemEval evaluation sets may contain similar contexts. The paper should validate that BERT sense labels are generated in a held-out manner or use context-dropping strategies to ensure independence.

- No convergence guarantee for iterative disambiguation: The iterative sense disambiguation process (Section 3.1) updates the context embedding using weighted sense embeddings (Eq. 5), which in turn affects sense probabilities—a cyclic dependency. However, the paper provides no analysis of whether this procedure converges or under what conditions it might oscillate. Without fixed-point analysis or empirical monitoring of convergence across iterations, the stability of the training process is questionable. At minimum, the authors should report the number of iterations used and whether convergence was observed in practice.

- Fixed number of senses limits expressiveness: The model assumes exactly three senses per word, which contradicts the known variability in polysemy across vocabulary (e.g., 'run' has many senses, 'triskaidekaphobia' has one). This fixed-K assumption likely hurts performance on words with fewer or more than three meanings and limits the model's ability to match BERT’s nuanced representations. While the conclusion mentions future work on tuning sense counts, the current design choice weakens the claim of 'state-of-the-art' performance, especially when compared to AdaGram, which infers K per word. The authors should either justify K=3 via ablation or report per-word analysis showing how well the three clusters align with human judgments.

- Evaluation omits domain adaptation and generalization: All experiments use Wikipedia-trained models evaluated on standard benchmarks, but no evaluation considers domain shift or adaptation. Given that BERT was pre-trained on diverse corpora including books and web text, and the model is trained on Wikipedia, it is unclear how well the distillation process generalizes to other domains. The paper misses an opportunity to test robustness on out-of-domain WSI tasks or with domain-specific vocabulary. For a method claiming practical utility in resource-constrained systems, domain generalization is a critical omission.

- Missing analysis of sense embedding quality: While the model induces senses and performs well on downstream tasks, the paper does not analyze what the learned sense embeddings represent. For a method claiming to extract discrete word senses from BERT, it is essential to verify that the three learned senses per word align with interpretable, semantically distinct meanings. A standard validation in this field is to inspect the nearest neighbors of each sense vector for a set of polysemous words (e.g., 'bank', 'crane', 'apple') and assess whether clusters correspond to distinct meanings (e.g., financial vs. river; bird vs. machine). The authors should add a qualitative analysis showing the top neighbors of each of the three sense vectors for 5–10 such words, using the BERTKDEmbed model, and discuss whether the learned senses are coherent and distinct.

- No comparison to direct BERT-based clustering: The paper positions itself as distilling discrete senses from BERT, but it does not compare against a simple baseline that clusters BERT’s contextual embeddings directly. For example, one could extract BERT embeddings for all instances of a word across contexts and apply k-means (k=3) to induce sense clusters, then evaluate those clusters on WSI tasks. This would establish whether the proposed two-stage method adds value over a straightforward clustering of BERT outputs. Given that the BERTSense model already computes sense probabilities from BERT embeddings, the comparison is natural. The authors should report ARI scores for this baseline on the SemEval datasets and discuss whether the additional complexity of training a student embedding model is justified.

- Downstream task uses only bag-of-senses: The embedded topic modeling experiment replaces each word with its most probable sense, discarding uncertainty and soft assignments. This limits the demonstration of the model’s advantages, as it does not leverage the full probabilistic sense distribution the model produces. A more faithful integration would weight topic assignments by sense probabilities or treat senses as latent variables in the generative process. The current approach reduces the benefit of multi-sense embeddings to a hard clustering step, which could be replicated with simpler models. The authors should compare their bag-of-senses ETM with a version that uses expected sense embeddings (i.e., $\sum_k p(s_k|w,C) \mathbf{v}_k$) as input and report whether this soft representation further improves perplexity or topic quality.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add an ablation study comparing BERTKDEmbed with its non-distilled counterpart under identical conditions to isolate the effect of knowledge distillation

- Address potential context leakage by re-running BERT sense extraction with context masking or using disjoint context splits for teacher and student

- Analyze or empirically verify convergence of the iterative disambiguation procedure and report the number of iterations used in training

- Justify the fixed three-sense assumption with ablation or per-word clustering analysis, or extend the model to variable K

- Evaluate model robustness on at least one out-of-domain or domain-shifted WSI benchmark

comments.title: Distillation loss uses incorrect direction for KL divergence
comments.quote:
text{transfer}}=-T^{2}\sum_{k=1}^{K}p_{\text{sense}}^{T}(w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}|w_{i},C)\log(p_{\text{BERT}}^{T}(w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}|w_{i},C))$ (10)

is
comments.explanation: The distillation loss in Equation (10) computes the cross-entropy with the student distribution in the log term, which reverses the standard direction of knowledge transfer. In proper distillation, the fixed teacher distribution should be the target, and the student's parameters should be updated to match it. The correct form is $-\sum_k p_{\text{BERT}}^T(k) \log p_{\text{sense}}^T(k)$, not the reverse. As written, the loss does not provide meaningful gradients to the student model because it treats the student’s output as fixed. This is a critical error in the formulation of the core distillation mechanism.

comments.title: Context vector in BERT sense model includes target word embedding
comments.quote:
where, context embedding

$\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}=\frac{1}{2\Delta}\sum_{l=-\Delta}^{\Delta}\mathbf{b}_{i+l}$
comments.explanation: The context vector $\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}$ includes $\mathbf{b}_i$, the BERT embedding of the target word, which creates a circular dependency: the sense probability for $w_i$ depends on $\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}$, which in turn depends on $\mathbf{b}_i$, while $\mathbf{b}_i$ is also the target in the reconstruction loss (Eq. 8). This allows the model to exploit self-information rather than genuine contextual disambiguation. Standard practice excludes the target token from context representations. The equation should sum over $l \neq 0$ to ensure independence between context and target.

comments.title: Loss function couples prediction and target via shared BERT embedding
comments.quote:
We want the weighted sum of the sense embeddings to be similar to the contextual embedding from BERT. So, the loss function is

$L_{\text{BERT}}=\sum_{w_{i}\in D}\left\|\sum_{k=1}^{K}p(w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}|w_{i},C)\mathbf{u}_{i}^{k}-\mathbf{b}_{i}\right\|_{2}^{2}$ (8)
comments.explanation: Equation (8) uses $\mathbf{b}_i$ both as the target and in computing the sense probabilities $p(w_i = w_i^k | w_i, C)$, since $\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}$ includes $\mathbf{b}_i$. This tight coupling risks degenerate solutions—e.g., concentrating all probability mass on a single sense that aligns with $\mathbf{b}_i$, regardless of semantic coherence. True distillation should decouple the teacher signal from the student’s internal inference. A better approach would use a context-only BERT representation (e.g., from masked inference) as the target to prevent information leakage.

comments.title: Sigmoid in prediction model does not define valid probability distribution
comments.quote:
Here the probability of a context word, $w_{i+l}$ given a sense embedding, $w_{i}^{k}$ is

$p(w_{i+l}|w_{i}=w_{i}^{k})=\sigma(\langle\mathbf{g}_{i+l},\mathbf{v}_{i}^{k}\rangle)$ (2)
comments.explanation: Equation (2) uses the sigmoid function to represent $p(w_{i+l}|w_i = w_i^k)$, but this scalar score cannot form a valid categorical distribution over the vocabulary. The expression $\sigma(\langle \mathbf{g}_{i+l}, \mathbf{v}_i^k \rangle)$ is unnormalized and does not sum to one across words. While negative sampling approximates the likelihood, presenting it as a generative probability in a mixture model (Eq. 1) is mathematically inconsistent. The model should either define a proper softmax or reframe (2) as a discriminative score used only in the negative sampling objective, with clarification in the text.

comments.title: Global embedding role is undefined for center word
comments.quote:
We also have a global embedding $\mathbf{g}_{i}$ for each word $w_{i}$.

Following the word2vec framework to train the sense embeddings, we slide a window of size $2\Delta+1$ (center word and $\Delta$ words to its left and right) over the dataset.
comments.explanation: The global embedding $\mathbf{g}_i$ is introduced for each word but is not used in any role for the center word $w_i$. It appears in the context embedding (Eq. 4) for surrounding words, but $\mathbf{g}_i$ itself is not involved in computing sense probabilities (Eq. 3) or predictions (Eq. 2). This raises ambiguity: if $\mathbf{g}_i$ represents the base identity of $w_i$, it should inform the initialization of $\mathbf{v}_i^k$ or $\mathbf{d}_i^k$, or act as a query in the attention mechanism. As written, its purpose is unclear and may be redundant.

comments.title: Context embedding update creates circular dependency
comments.quote:
Step 2 Replace the global embeddings in Eq. 4 with the weighted average of the sense embeddings for each context word

$\mathbf{c}=\frac{1}{2\Delta}\sum_{\stackrel{l=-\Delta}{l\neq 0}}^{\Delta}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathbf{v}_{i+l}^{k}p(w_{i+l}=w_{i+l}^{k}|w_{i+l},C)$ (5)
comments.explanation: Equation (5) updates the context embedding using sense probabilities that depend on the current context, creating a cyclic dependency: sense probabilities depend on $\mathbf{c}$, which now depends on those same probabilities. The paper provides no analysis of convergence or iteration schedule. Without explicit initialization, update rules, and stopping criteria, the iterative process is underspecified and risks instability. The model should clarify whether this is a single refinement step or multiple iterations, and report empirical convergence behavior.

comments.title: AvgSimC formula incorrectly normalized by $1/K^2$
comments.explanation: The factor $\frac{1}{K^2}$ in Equation (11) is unjustified. The sense probabilities $p(w_1 = w_1^i | w_1, C)$ and $p(w_2 = w_2^j | w_2, C)$ are already normalized, so their product forms a valid joint distribution over sense pairs. The expected cosine similarity should be computed as $\sum_{i,j} p(s_i|w_1,C) p(s_j|w_2,C) \cos(v_i, v_j)$ without additional scaling. Including $\frac{1}{K^2}$ artificially suppresses the score, especially for $K=3$, and invalidates comparisons across models with different $K$.

comments.title: Distillation loss justification misstates gradient scaling
comments.quote: We multiply this loss by $T^{2}$ because the magnitudes of the gradients in the distillation loss scale by $1/T^{2}$
comments.explanation: The justification for multiplying the distillation loss by $T^2$ is incorrect. While the effective signal strength in temperature-scaled distillation is reduced, the gradient of the cross-entropy loss with respect to the student logits scales as $1/T$, not $1/T^2$. The $T^2$ factor is empirically useful to balance loss magnitudes, but the stated reason misrepresents the underlying calculus. The paper should revise the explanation to reflect that $T^2$ compensates for the softened gradients due to temperature scaling, without claiming an exact $1/T^2$ scaling of first-order derivatives.

comments.title: Student temperature-scaled distribution is undefined
comments.quote:
text{transfer}}=-T^{2}\sum_{k=1}^{K}p_{\text{sense}}^{T}(w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}|w_{i},C)\log(p_{\text{BERT}}^{T}(w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}|w_{i},C))$ (10)

is
comments.explanation: The term $p_{\text{sense}}^{T}$ appears without definition. The student’s sense probabilities in Eq. (3) are defined using a standard softmax, not a temperature-scaled one. For distillation to work properly, the student should also use a softened output distribution during training. Otherwise, comparing a softened teacher to a sharp student breaks the symmetry of the KL divergence. The paper must define $p_{\text{sense}}^{T}$ as $\mathrm{softmax}(\mathbf{z}_{\text{sense}} / T)$ and clarify that the student uses temperature scaling during distillation.

comments.title: Sense scoring vectors $\mathbf{d}_i^k$ are not defined in BERT sense model
comments.quote:
Similar to Eq. 3 the probability of sense, $k$ of word, $w_{i}$ in context $C$ is

$p(w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}|w_{i},C)=\frac{\exp(\langle\mathbf{d}_{i}^{k},\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}\rangle)}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}\exp(\langle\mathbf{d}_{i}^{k},\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}\rangle)}$ (7)
comments.explanation:
The vectors $\mathbf{d}_i^k$ used in Eq. (7) are not defined in the BERT sense model section. The paper states that $\{\mathbf{u}_i^1, \ldots, 
\mathbf{u}_i^K\}$ are the sense embeddings trained from BERT, but it does not specify whether $\mathbf{d}_i^k$ are equal to $\mathbf{u}_i^k$, a learned transformation, or independent parameters. This omission makes the computation of sense probabilities ambiguous and hinders reproducibility. The relationship between $\mathbf{d}_i^k$ and $\mathbf{u}_i^k$ must be explicitly stated.

comments.title: WordPiece averaging may dilute sense-specific signals
comments.quote: To get the contextual embedding $\mathbf{b}_{i}$ from these token embeddings, we take the mean of all token output embeddings for a word.
comments.explanation: Averaging all WordPiece token embeddings for a word conflates potentially distinct subword-level signals before disambiguation. For polysemous words like 'bank', different subword contexts (e.g., 'bank account' vs. 'river bank') might carry sense-specific information that is lost in the mean. A more principled approach would use attention over subword embeddings conditioned on context to preserve fine-grained distinctions during sense induction.

comments.title: Bag-of-sense representation introduces unfair advantage in topic modeling
comments.quote: During training using our sense embeddings and the MSSG multi-sense embeddings, the sentences are represented using a "bag-of-sense" representation where each word is replaced with its most probable sense, selected using a context window of 10 words; with the skip-gram embeddings, bag-of-words representations are used.
comments.explanation: The use of context-dependent bag-of-sense representation for multi-sense models, while using static bag-of-words for skip-gram, creates an architectural asymmetry. The performance gain could stem from the dynamic disambiguation mechanism rather than the embedding structure itself. To isolate the effect of multi-sense representations, all models should use the same input encoding—either context-based disambiguation for all or none.

comments.title: Normalization of perplexity by vocabulary size lacks justification
comments.quote: In Figure 2, we show the ratio of the document completion perplexity (normalized by vocabulary size) and the topic quality.
comments.explanation: Normalizing perplexity by vocabulary size is not standard and lacks theoretical grounding. Perplexity is already a per-token measure; dividing it further by vocabulary size distorts comparisons across different vocabulary settings. This artificial normalization may bias results, especially when vocabulary size varies due to frequency thresholds. The correct practice is to report raw perplexity or perplexity per word, as in the original ETM work.

comments.title: Mapping from sense embeddings to word-level ETM inputs is unspecified
comments.quote: ETM is initialized with pre-trained sense embeddings and then fine tuned along with the topic embeddings.
comments.explanation: The ETM assumes one embedding per word, but the input provides three sense embeddings per word. The paper does not specify how these are combined into the word-level embedding matrix $\rho$. Without this detail—e.g., averaging, selection, or concatenation—the initialization is ambiguous and the model cannot be reproduced. A clear specification is essential for transparency.

comments.title: Summing sense probabilities post-softmax is not valid without vocabulary expansion
comments.quote: We sum the probability for the different senses of a word to obtain the probability for a word.
comments.explanation: The model sums probabilities over senses after a softmax that appears to be defined over words, not senses. This operation is only valid if the output vocabulary includes distinct entries for each sense. Otherwise, summing post-softmax breaks the probabilistic structure. The paper must clarify whether the vocabulary is expanded to include sense-specific tokens and how normalization is handled.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a knowledge distillation method from BERT to create multi-sense word embeddings. The approach uses a two-stage process: first learning sense embeddings from BERT's contextual representations, then distilling this knowledge into a smaller skip-gram-like model. While the method shows improvements on word sense induction tasks, there are significant methodological gaps and unexamined assumptions that weaken the paper's contributions.

The paper presents an interesting approach to extracting multi-sense embeddings from BERT through knowledge distillation. The method demonstrates clear improvements on word sense induction tasks when using distillation, and the downstream topic modeling application provides evidence of practical utility. However, the paper suffers from insufficient methodological detail, particularly around the distillation process and sense selection mechanism, and makes several unexamined assumptions about BERT's sense representations.

Overview issues:

- Underspecified Knowledge Distillation Process: The paper lacks critical details about the knowledge distillation implementation. Section 3.2.2 mentions using temperature T=4 and cross-entropy loss, but fails to specify what exactly is being distilled - are the logits from BERT's sense attention mechanism (Eq. 7) being used directly, or are these computed separately? The paper also doesn't explain how the sense probabilities from BERT are obtained for the distillation loss. Without these details, the method is not reproducible and it's unclear what knowledge is actually being transferred from BERT to the student model.

- Unexamined Assumption About BERT's Sense Representations: The paper assumes BERT's contextual embeddings naturally align with discrete word senses without validation. Section 3.2.1 treats BERT embeddings as ground truth for sense induction, but there's no analysis of whether BERT actually produces sense-distinguishable representations. The authors should provide evidence (e.g., clustering BERT embeddings for polysemous words) demonstrating that BERT's output space contains meaningful sense distinctions before using it as a teacher. Without this validation, the distillation may be transferring noise rather than semantic knowledge.

- Fixed Number of Senses Per Word: The method uses a fixed K=3 senses for all words (Section 4.1), which is a significant limitation. Words vary greatly in their degree of polysemy - some have many senses while others are monosemous. The paper acknowledges this as future work but doesn't discuss the impact of this constraint on results. For example, how does the model handle words with more than 3 dominant senses in the evaluation datasets? This design choice could artificially limit performance on words requiring more sense distinctions.

- Missing Ablation Study on Iterative Disambiguation: Section 3.1 describes an iterative sense disambiguation mechanism (Eq. 5) that uses sense embeddings to compute context embeddings, but the experiments never isolate its contribution. The authors should compare the full model against a version using only global embeddings for context (Eq. 4). Without this ablation, it's unclear whether the iterative process is necessary or whether most gains come from the BERT distillation itself. This is particularly important given the computational overhead of the iterative approach.

- Inconsistent Performance Across Evaluation Tasks: The model shows contradictory performance patterns across tasks. In Table 2, BERTsense (768D) outperforms BERTKDEmbed (300D) on WSI, but in Table 5, BERTKDEmbed significantly outperforms BERTsense on contextual similarity. The paper claims this is because BERTsense embeddings are "not optimal for training sense embeddings" (Section 4.5), but doesn't explain why the distillation process would improve sense representation while hurting sense selection. This inconsistency needs deeper analysis - perhaps the distillation loss over-optimizes for similarity at the expense of discriminability.

- Limited Analysis of Computational Efficiency: Despite claiming efficiency advantages over BERT, the paper provides minimal analysis of computational costs. Section 4.1 mentions 10 hours for one epoch on a V100, but doesn't compare this to baseline training times or analyze the two-stage training overhead. The distillation process itself requires running BERT inference, which may negate some efficiency gains. A proper efficiency evaluation should include: (1) training time comparison with baselines, (2) inference speed benchmarks, and (3) memory usage analysis to substantiate the efficiency claims.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous result: The paper needs to show that its knowledge distillation method actually transfers meaningful sense distinctions from BERT. A concrete demonstration would involve taking a polysemous word like 'bank', extracting BERT embeddings for contexts representing its different meanings (river bank vs financial institution), clustering these embeddings to show they form distinct groups, and then demonstrating that the distilled student model successfully recovers these same sense clusters. Without showing that BERT's output space contains recoverable sense structure, the distillation may be transferring noise rather than semantic knowledge.

- No worked example of distillation mechanics: The paper lacks a concrete, step-by-step illustration of how the distillation process works for a specific example. The authors should walk through a complete mini-example: select a target word and its context, show the BERT contextual embedding computation, demonstrate how the BERT Sense Model generates sense probabilities (Eq. 7), and then show how the student model's sense probabilities (Eq. 3) are aligned with the teacher through the distillation loss (Eq. 10). This would clarify what exactly is being distilled and help readers understand the mechanics of the two-stage approach.

- Unverified claim about downstream task benefits: The paper claims improved performance in embedded topic modeling but doesn't adequately demonstrate this advantage stems from the multi-sense nature of the embeddings. A proper demonstration would include: (1) showing specific topics where sense disambiguation makes a difference (e.g., topics containing 'apple' where food and technology meanings are separated), (2) comparing against a strong baseline that uses contextual BERT embeddings directly for topic modeling, and (3) analyzing whether the improvements persist when controlling for embedding dimensionality. Without this analysis, the topic modeling results don't convincingly demonstrate the value of the proposed distillation approach.

- Missing implementation guidance for K selection: While the paper acknowledges that fixing K=3 for all words is a limitation, it provides no practical guidance for how practitioners could determine appropriate K values for different words. The authors should include a concrete method for selecting K, such as using the entropy of BERT's sense distribution or analyzing the clustering structure of BERT embeddings for each word. They should demonstrate this method on examples of monosemous words (where K=1 would be appropriate) and highly polysemous words (where K>3 might be needed), showing how dynamic K selection affects performance on the evaluation tasks.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide complete implementation details of the knowledge distillation process, including how BERT sense probabilities are computed and what specific representations are distilled

- Add analysis validating that BERT embeddings contain meaningful sense distinctions before using them as a teacher model

- Include ablation study on the iterative disambiguation mechanism to isolate its contribution from the distillation gains

- Analyze the impact of using a fixed number of senses per word and discuss limitations for highly polysemous words

- Add comprehensive computational efficiency analysis with training time, inference speed, and memory usage comparisons

comments.title: Incorrect task definition for word sense induction
comments.quote: In word sense induction, a model has to select the correct meaning of a word given its context.
comments.explanation: This definition is incorrect for word sense induction (WSI). WSI is an unsupervised clustering task where the model must group word usages by meaning without predefined sense inventories. The description given actually matches word sense disambiguation (WSD), which uses predefined sense inventories. This is a fundamental distinction in the field. The paper's own evaluation in Section 4.3 uses clustering metrics (ARI), which confirms WSI is the correct task. Rewrite as "In word sense induction, a model must group word usages by meaning without predefined sense inventories, typically by clustering contextual representations."

comments.title: Triangle inequality explanation is mathematically incorrect
comments.quote: For apple, the distance between the words similar to its two meanings, i.e. peach and software, will be less than the sum of their distances to apple.
comments.explanation: The triangle inequality states that for any three points, the distance between any two points is always less than or equal to the sum of the distances from those points to a third point. The paper presents this as a limitation, but it's actually a fundamental geometric property that holds for all valid distance metrics. If peach and software are both close to apple in the embedding space, then by definition their distance to each other must be less than or equal to d(peach,apple) + d(software,apple). This isn't a problem to be solved but rather a mathematical necessity. The authors should remove this explanation or clarify that the issue is semantic (unrelated meanings becoming close) rather than mathematical.

comments.title: Loss function uses undefined conditional probability
comments.quote:
The loss function for the multi-sense embedding model is as follows

\[ L_{\text{sense}}=-\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}w_{i}\in D\end{subarray}}\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}l=-\Delta\\
l\neq 0\end{subarray}}^{\Delta}\{\log[p(w_{i+l}|w_{i})]+\sum_{j=1}^{n}\log[1-p(w_{i+l,j}|w_{i})]\} \] (6)
comments.explanation: Equation 6 uses $p(w_{i+l}|w_{i})$ but this probability is not defined in the multi-sense context established in the paper. The paper introduces sense probabilities $p(k|w,C)$ but doesn't specify how to compute the probability of a context word given a target word. In multi-sense models, this should be derived by marginalizing over senses: $p(w_{i+l}|w_{i}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} p(k|w_{i},C) \cdot p(w_{i+l}|\mathbf{v}_{i}^{k})$, where $p(w_{i+l}|\mathbf{v}_{i}^{k})$ is computed using the k-th sense embedding of $w_i$. Add this definition before Equation 6 because the loss function cannot be properly interpreted or implemented without this specification.

comments.title: MaxSimC equation missing probability weighting
comments.quote:
The MaxSimC method selects the best sense for each context based on their probabilities and measures their cosine similarity.

$$
\operatorname {M a x S i m C} \left(w _ {1}, w _ {2}\right) = \cos \left(v _ {1} ^ {i}, v _ {2} ^ {j}\right) \tag {12}
$$

where

$$
i = \arg \max  _ {k = 1} ^ {K} p \left(w _ {1} = w _ {1} ^ {k} \mid w _ {1}, C\right) \quad \text {and} \quad j = \arg \max  _ {k = 1} ^ {K} p \left(w _ {2} = w _ {2} ^ {k} \mid w _ {2}, C\right) \tag {13}
$$
comments.explanation: The MaxSimC formulation is incomplete. While it correctly identifies the most probable senses for each word, it should weight the cosine similarity by the product of these probabilities to reflect confidence in the sense selection. The current equation treats all MaxSimC scores equally regardless of how certain the model is about the selected senses. This creates an inconsistency with AvgSimC, which properly incorporates probability weights. The corrected MaxSimC should be: MaxSimC(w₁,w₂) = p(w₁=w₁ⁱ|w₁,C) × p(w₂=w₂ʲ|w₂,C) × cos(v₁ⁱ,v₂ʲ), where i and j are defined as in Eq. 13. This ensures both methods account for sense probability confidence.

comments.title: Inconsistent model dimensionality claim in Table 2
comments.quote: Table 2: ARI (Adjusted Rand Index) scores for word sense induction tasks. All models have dimension of 300. The Adagram and Disambiguated skip-gram models learn variable number of senses while all other models learn fixed number of senses per word. All baseline results except MUSE were collected from Grzegorczyk & Kurdziel (2018). SenseEmbed - Sense embedding model without distillation, BERTsense - Sense embedding model (Sec. 3.2.1), BERTKDEmbed - Sense embedding with distillation
comments.explanation:
The caption incorrectly claims all models have 300 dimensions. BERTsense uses BERT's 768-dimensional embeddings as the teacher without distillation, so it should have 768 dimensions, not 300. The caption should specify which models have 300 dimensions and acknowledge BERTsense's different dimensionality.

Rewrite the caption as: "Table 2: ARI (Adjusted Rand Index) scores for word sense induction tasks. All models except BERTsense have dimension of 300. BERTsense uses 768-dimensional BERT embeddings. The Adagram and Disambiguated skip-gram models learn variable number of senses while all other models learn fixed number of senses per word. All baseline results except MUSE were collected from Grzegorczyk & Kurdziel (2018). SenseEmbed - Sense embedding model without distillation, BERTsense - Sense embedding model (Sec. 3.2.1), BERTKDEmbed - Sense embedding with distillation"

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in improvement claim
comments.quote: We can see a significant improvement in ARI score  $(1.25x - 2.25x)$  on the 3 datasets with knowledge distillation from BERT.
comments.explanation:
The claimed improvement range is incorrect. Comparing BERTKDEmbed (with distillation) to SenseEmbed (without distillation): SE-2007: 0.145/0.053 = 2.74x, SE-2010: 0.144/0.076 = 1.89x, SE-2013: 0.133/0.054 = 2.46x. The actual improvements range from 1.89x to 2.74x, not 1.25x to 2.25x.

Rewrite as: "We can see a significant improvement in ARI score $(1.89x - 2.74x)$ on the 3 datasets with knowledge distillation from BERT."

comments.title: Undefined context embedding variable in Equation 7
comments.quote:
Similar to Eq. 3 the probability of sense, $k$ of word, $w_{i}$ in context $C$ is

$p(w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}|w_{i},C)=\frac{\exp(\langle\mathbf{d}_{i}^{k},\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}\rangle)}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}\exp(\langle\mathbf{d}_{i}^{k},\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}\rangle)}$ (7)
comments.explanation: Equation 7 introduces the variable $\mathbf{d}_{i}^{k}$ without definition, while the preceding text defines sense embeddings as $\{\mathbf{u}_{i}^{1},\ldots,\mathbf{u}_{i}^{K}\}$. This creates a mathematical inconsistency since the probability calculation depends on an undefined variable. Rewrite equation 7 as $p(w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}|w_{i},C)=\frac{\exp(\langle\mathbf{u}_{i}^{k},\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}\rangle)}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}\exp(\langle\mathbf{u}_{i}^{k},\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}\rangle)}$ because the sense embeddings $\mathbf{u}_{i}^{k}$ are the only defined sense representations in this section.

comments.title: Missing cosine distance computation details
comments.quote: The most probable sense is selected and then the cosine distance is computed with the global embeddings of all words in the vocabulary.
comments.explanation: The text doesn't specify which embedding is used for the cosine distance computation. Is it the selected sense embedding, the context embedding, or some combination? This ambiguity makes it impossible to replicate the nearest neighbor computation. Add clarification: "The cosine distance is computed between the selected sense embedding and the global embeddings of all words in the vocabulary."

comments.title: Undefined global embeddings reference
comments.quote: The cosine distance is computed with the global embeddings of all words in the vocabulary.
comments.explanation: The paper hasn't defined what "global embeddings" are or how they differ from sense embeddings. Are these pre-trained BERT embeddings, learned separately, or aggregated from sense embeddings? This term needs explicit definition. Add after the quoted text: "Global embeddings refer to the context-independent word embeddings learned during training, distinct from the context-dependent sense embeddings."

comments.title: Inconsistent summation index in context embedding
comments.quote:
where, $\mathbf{c}$ is the context embedding for context $C$ defined by the average of the global embeddings of the context words

$\mathbf{c}=\frac{1}{2\Delta}\sum_{\stackrel{{\scriptstyle t=-\Delta}}{{l\neq 0}}}^{\Delta}\mathbf{g}_{i+l}$ (4)
comments.explanation:
The summation notation in Equation 4 contains an inconsistency: the summation index is written as $t$ in the subscript $t=-\Delta$, but the condition $l\neq 0$ and the term $\mathbf{g}_{i+l}$ both use $l$. This creates ambiguity about which index variable is being used. Since $l$ is used consistently throughout the section (e.g., in $w_{i+l}$ and $l\in[-\Delta,\Delta]$), the summation should use $l$ throughout.

Rewrite Equation 4 as $\mathbf{c}=\frac{1}{2\Delta}\sum_{\stackrel{{\scriptstyle l=-\Delta}}{{l\neq 0}}}^{\Delta}\mathbf{g}_{i+l}$ to maintain consistent notation with the rest of the section.

comments.title: Undefined context embedding in attention mechanism
comments.quote: $p(w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}|w_{i},C)=\frac{\exp(\langle\mathbf{d}_{i}^{k},\mathbf{c}\rangle)}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}\exp(\langle\mathbf{d}_{i}^{k},\mathbf{c}\rangle)}$ (3)
comments.explanation:
Equation 3 uses the context embedding $\mathbf{c}$ in the attention mechanism, but $\mathbf{c}$ is not defined until Equation 4. This creates a forward reference issue where a variable is used before its definition. The logical flow would be clearer if Equation 4 (defining $\mathbf{c}$) appeared before Equation 3.

Move Equation 4 and its explanatory text to appear before Equation 3, or add a forward reference: 'where $\mathbf{c}$ is the context embedding defined below in Equation 4'.

comments.title: Ambiguous probability notation in Equation 1
comments.explanation:
The notation $w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}$ in Equation 1 is ambiguous. It appears to denote 'word $w_i$ takes on its $k$-th sense', but this could be misinterpreted as an equality between two different word tokens. Since $w_i^{k}$ is not defined elsewhere as a notation for the $k$-th sense of $w_i$, this creates confusion.

Replace $w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}$ with clearer notation such as $\text{sense}(w_i)=k$ or define $w_i^{k}$ explicitly as 'the $k$-th sense of word $w_i$' before Equation 1.

comments.title: Inconsistent probability notation in equation
comments.quote:
Using the context embedding from Eq. 4, compute the sense probability $p(w_{i+l}=w_{i+l}^{k}|w_{i+l},C)$ for each word in the context ($l\in[-\Delta,\Delta],l\neq 0$

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

Rep 2 Replace the global embeddings in Eq. 4 with the weighted average of the sense embeddings for each context word

\[ \mathbf{c}=\frac{1}{2\Delta}\sum_{\begin{subarray}{c}l=-\Delta\\
l\neq 0\end{subarray}}^{\Delta}\sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathbf{v}_{i+l}^{k}\,p(w_{i+l}=w_{i+l}^{k}|w_{i+l},C) \] (5)
comments.explanation: The notation $p(w_{i+l}=w_{i+l}^{k}|w_{i+l},C)$ is indeed confusing. The expression $w_{i+l}=w_{i+l}^{k}$ suggests an equality between a word and its sense embedding, which are different mathematical objects. In multi-sense embedding literature, the standard notation for sense probability is $p(k|w_{i+l},C)$, representing the probability of the k-th sense given the word and context. Rewrite the probability notation as $p(k|w_{i+l},C)$ in both the text and Equation 5 because this notation is mathematically precise and aligns with standard conventions in multi-sense embedding models.

comments.title: Undefined context window parameter
comments.quote:
where, context embedding

$\mathbf{c}_{\text{BERT}}=\frac{1}{2\Delta}\sum_{l=-\Delta}^{\Delta}\mathbf{b}_{i+l}$
comments.explanation: The parameter $\Delta$ is introduced without definition or explanation of how it's determined. This is critical for reproducibility since it defines the context window size. Add a definition after the equation: "where $\Delta$ is the context window half-size (e.g., $\Delta=2$ for a 5-word context window) and is a hyperparameter of the model."

comments.title: Undefined dataset notation
comments.quote: $L_{\text{BERT}}=\sum_{w_{i}\in D}\left\|\sum_{k=1}^{K}p(w_{i}=w_{i}^{k}|w_{i},C)\mathbf{u}_{i}^{k}-\mathbf{b}_{i}\right\|_{2}^{2}$ (8)
comments.explanation: The notation $D$ is used in the summation without definition. Readers cannot determine what constitutes the training set or how words are sampled. Add clarification before equation 8: "where $D$ is the training dataset containing word-context pairs $(w_i, C)$ and the summation is over all word instances in the training corpus."

comments.title: Inconsistent equation reference
comments.quote:
et. The weight for the knowledge distillation loss (Eq. 9) $\alpha=1$ is selected from {0.1, 0.5, 1.0}. Temperature parameter $T$ is set to 4 by tuning over the range [2,6].

### 4
comments.explanation: The text references Eq. 9 for the knowledge distillation loss, but the paper-level issues mention Eq. 10 as the distillation loss equation. This creates a cross-reference inconsistency. The authors should verify which equation number is correct and ensure consistent referencing throughout the paper.

comments.title: Typo in performance claim
comments.quote: We demonstrate start-of-the-art results on three word sense induction data sets using fast and efficient sense embedding models.
comments.explanation: There is a typographical error where "start-of-the-art" should be "state-of-the-art." While this appears to be a simple typo, it affects the credibility of the performance claim in the conclusion.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a two-stage knowledge distillation approach to transfer sense-aware information from a pre-trained BERT model into static, multi-sense skip-gram embeddings. The authors aim to improve efficiency in resource-constrained environments while maintaining performance on word sense induction and contextual similarity tasks.

The paper addresses a practical and relevant problem in lexical semantics by bridging the gap between high-performing but computationally expensive contextual models and efficient static embeddings. While the performance gains on SemEval benchmarks are promising, the methodology requires more rigorous justification regarding the preservation of semantic nuance during distillation.

Overview issues:

- Loss of semantic nuance in distillation: The methodology assumes that distilling BERT's hidden states via MSE (Eq. 8) and cross-entropy (Eq. 10) preserves the latent sense structure of the teacher. However, the paper lacks a qualitative analysis or sensitivity study to determine if rare or domain-specific senses are 'washed out' by the student model's fixed capacity. Given that the student model is restricted to a fixed number of senses (K=3), it is unclear how the model handles the long-tail distribution of polysemy compared to the teacher. The authors should include an ablation or a case study comparing the student's sense clusters against the teacher's attention heads to verify if the distillation process successfully captures the intended semantic granularity.

- Sensitivity to fixed sense counts: The model assumes a fixed number of senses (K=3) for all words, which contradicts the known reality of varying polysemy across the lexicon. Section 4.1 mentions this choice for comparison with prior work, but this limitation is not adequately addressed in the discussion of results. A fixed K-sense architecture likely forces the model to conflate distinct senses for highly polysemous words while over-partitioning monosemous ones. The authors should provide a discussion on how this constraint impacts performance on the SemEval-2013 dataset, which specifically targets graded and non-graded senses, and perhaps perform a sensitivity analysis on the choice of K.

- Incomplete evaluation of sense disambiguation: The evaluation relies heavily on ARI scores for clustering, but the paper does not sufficiently decouple the performance of the sense selection mechanism (Eq. 3) from the quality of the sense embeddings themselves. Because the model uses a simple dot-product attention mechanism, it is possible that the improvements in ARI are driven more by the context-window averaging than by the quality of the distilled sense representation. It would be beneficial to include a baseline that uses the same architecture but is trained without the knowledge distillation loss, specifically to isolate the contribution of the BERT-derived soft labels versus the architectural design of the sense disambiguation module.

- Lack of statistical significance testing: The reported ARI improvements (e.g., Table 2) are presented as absolute values without confidence intervals or significance tests. Given the noise inherent in WSI tasks and the dependence on random initialization in skip-gram frameworks, it is difficult to determine if the reported gains are robust or artifacts of hyperparameter tuning. The authors should perform multiple runs with different random seeds and report the mean and standard deviation for the primary metrics to ensure the distillation benefits are statistically meaningful.

- Lack of evidence for distillation of rare or domain-specific senses: The paper claims that knowledge distillation transfers contextual information from BERT to static sense embeddings, but it does not demonstrate if this transfer is uniform across the frequency spectrum. It is highly probable that the distillation process prioritizes common senses while discarding rare or domain-specific meanings due to the fixed K=3 capacity constraint. To address this, the authors should provide a case study or a targeted evaluation on a subset of words known to have high polysemy or rare senses (e.g., using the WordNet-based sense distributions). Showing how the distilled model performs on these specific subsets compared to the teacher would verify if the distillation is truly capturing semantic nuance or merely learning the most frequent sense.

- Missing ablation on the impact of the teacher model's layer selection: The methodology computes the teacher's contextual embedding using the output layer of BERT. However, it is well-documented that different layers of BERT capture different types of information, with lower layers often focusing on syntax and higher layers on semantics. The paper lacks an analysis or justification for why the final layer is sufficient for distilling sense-specific information. An ablation study comparing the performance of the student model when distilled from different BERT layers (e.g., layer 6 vs. layer 12) would significantly strengthen the claim that the distillation process is capturing relevant semantic structure.

- Absence of a baseline comparison to an 'oracle' static embedding model: The paper compares the proposed method against several existing multi-sense embedding models, but it does not compare against a static embedding model initialized with BERT embeddings without the distillation training process. This makes it difficult to distinguish whether the performance gains are due to the distillation framework itself or simply the superior quality of the BERT-derived initialization. Including a baseline where the sense embeddings are initialized using BERT's average embedding for a word, followed by standard skip-gram training without the distillation loss, would clarify the specific contribution of the proposed distillation objective.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a compelling application of knowledge distillation, but it lacks the necessary rigorous analysis to prove that the distilled embeddings truly capture the semantic nuance of the teacher model. The authors must address the limitations of the fixed-sense assumption and provide statistical validation for their performance claims.

Revision targets:

- Perform statistical significance testing (e.g., multiple runs with different seeds) for the results in Table 2 and Table 3.

- Add a qualitative analysis or case study comparing the student model's sense clusters to the teacher's internal representations to demonstrate what semantic information is preserved.

- Provide a detailed discussion or sensitivity analysis regarding the fixed-sense assumption (K=3) and its impact on words with varying levels of polysemy.

- Include an ablation study that isolates the contribution of the knowledge distillation loss versus the base architecture.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of word sense induction
comments.quote: In word sense induction, a model has to select the correct meaning of a word given its context.
comments.explanation: The definition provided describes word sense disambiguation (WSD), not word sense induction (WSI). WSI requires discovering senses from data without a predefined inventory, whereas selecting from a known set is WSD. Given that the paper uses clustering-based metrics (ARI) standard for WSI, please revise this definition to clarify that the task involves grouping occurrences into induced clusters.

comments.title: Missing formal model description
comments.quote: We first describe our word sense embedding model. Then the details of extracting knowledge from the contextual embeddings in BERT are discussed.
comments.explanation: The methodology section is missing the formal definition of the model architecture, the objective function, and the structural constraints (such as the K=3 sense limit). Please provide the formal definitions of the sense embedding space and the distillation objective function to ensure the work is reproducible.

comments.title: Misleading reporting of ARI performance
comments.quote: 768). From Table 4, we see that the 300 dimensional sense embedding model can obtain  $75\%$  to  $88\%$  ARI scores without using the 12 layer transformer model with 768 dimensional embeddings.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'obtain 75% to 88% ARI scores' is misleading because the actual ARI scores in Table 4 are between 0.133 and 0.195. These percentages appear to be relative performance ratios compared to the teacher model. Please clarify that these figures represent relative performance to avoid confusion with absolute ARI metrics.

comments.title: Inconsistent improvement factors
comments.quote: We can see a significant improvement in ARI score  $(1.25x - 2.25x)$  on the 3 datasets with knowledge distillation from BERT.
comments.explanation: The reported improvement range of 1.25x to 2.25x does not match the data in Table 2. Calculating the ratio between the best baseline (Disamb. SG) and the proposed model (BERTKDEmbed) yields ratios up to 3.44x for the SemEval-2013 dataset. Please update the text to accurately reflect the performance gains calculated from your results table.

comments.title: Mathematical imprecision regarding triangle inequality
comments.quote: mbeddings. This leads to another limitation in the word embeddings due to the triangle inequality of vectors *(Neelakantan et al., 2014)*. For apple, the distance between the words similar to its two meanings, i.e. peach and software, will be less than the sum of their distances to apple. To account fo
comments.explanation: The description of the triangle inequality limitation is imprecise. The issue is that a single vector representation forces a geometric configuration that contradicts the semantic distance between distinct senses, rather than the triangle inequality itself being the limitation. Please rewrite this to clarify that the single-vector representation forces a geometric conflation that fails to satisfy expected semantic distances.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a two-stage knowledge distillation framework to transfer word sense information from BERT into static multi-sense embeddings. The methodology combines an MSE alignment objective with a softened cross-entropy distillation loss to improve sense disambiguation and contextual similarity. Experiments report improved ARI scores on SemEval WSI datasets and competitive performance on the SCWS similarity benchmark, alongside a downstream embedded topic modeling application. While the core idea of distilling sense boundaries into efficient static vectors is promising, the experimental design and evaluation pipeline contain several structural weaknesses that limit the validity of the performance claims.

The paper tackles a well-motivated problem: compressing contextual sense information into lightweight static embeddings for low-latency deployment. The two-stage distillation pipeline is clearly described, and the downstream topic modeling experiment adds practical value. However, the fixed sense count assumption, omitted computational overhead, and lack of statistical validation significantly weaken the empirical claims. The work shows potential but requires methodological tightening and more rigorous evaluation before it can be considered for publication.

Overview issues:

- Fixed Sense Count Restricts Induction Performance: The model enforces exactly K=3 senses per word throughout training and evaluation. Word sense induction datasets naturally contain words with two to five or more distinct meanings. Forcing a fixed cluster count artificially caps performance and distorts the Adjusted Rand Index scores reported in Table 2. This design choice also breaks the apples-to-apples comparison with variable-sense baselines like AdaGram and Disambiguated Skip-gram, which adapt their cluster numbers to each word. The claim of state-of-the-art performance becomes difficult to verify when the evaluation setup penalizes polysemous words with high sense counts. I recommend implementing a non-parametric sense discovery mechanism, such as an entropy-based pruning step or a Dirichlet Process prior, and reporting results across a range of K values to demonstrate stability.

- Training Pipeline Contradicts Efficiency Motivation: The introduction frames static embeddings as a practical alternative for resource-constrained systems and low-latency inference. The distillation procedure, however, requires running the full BERT-base model over a 990-million-word Wikipedia corpus to generate teacher embeddings before the student model even begins training. The authors only report a ten-hour-per-epoch cost for the student network while completely omitting the BERT inference overhead. This upfront computational burden directly negates the latency and memory benefits that justify the proposed approach. Readers will rightly question whether the distillation trade-off actually benefits deployment pipelines. The revision should quantify total training FLOPs and memory footprint, or switch to a lightweight teacher like DistilBERT to align the methodology with the stated efficiency goals.

- Unsubstantiated Performance Gains on Contextual Similarity: Section 4.4 claims that knowledge distillation significantly improves MaxSimC scores, yet Table 3 shows the distilled model scoring 67.2 against a 900D Skip-gram baseline at 68.4. The AvgSimC improvement over MUSE is equally marginal, sitting at 68.6 versus 68.8. The authors present these results without standard deviations or statistical significance tests. Without variance estimates, the claimed superiority remains speculative and fails to account for random initialization effects. The model also struggles to outperform a simple single-sense embedding that merely increases its dimensionality. I suggest reporting results over multiple random seeds, running paired statistical tests against all baselines, and honestly discussing the conditions under which the distillation objective actually helps.

- MSE Alignment Objective Risks Representation Collapse: Equation 8 minimizes the squared distance between a weighted sum of K static vectors and the 768-dimensional contextual output from BERT. This mathematical setup forces a small set of fixed prototypes to approximate a highly variable, context-dependent distribution. Such alignment objectives typically lead to representational collapse or over-smoothing, where distinct senses merge into a single averaged vector. If the distilled embeddings lose fine-grained discriminative features, the subsequent cross-entropy distillation in Equation 10 will merely transfer blurred probabilities rather than structured sense boundaries. The paper provides no analysis of the embedding space geometry to verify that distinct senses remain separable. Adding an ablation that measures nearest-neighbor sense purity or cosine similarity distributions would clarify whether the alignment step preserves meaningful semantic distinctions.

- Clustering Evaluation Lacks Sensitivity Analysis: The word sense induction evaluation assigns each context to the single highest-probability sense before computing clustering metrics. This hard assignment strategy is highly sensitive to temperature scaling, initialization, and the treatment of low-confidence predictions. The authors do not analyze how the model handles rare senses or long-tail polysemy distributions during the induction phase. WSI benchmarks are notoriously sensitive to clustering thresholds, and ignoring this variable masks potential overfitting to dominant senses.
